steps.title: Solve both quadratics using the quadratic formula
steps.content:
Use the discriminant when the question is really asking how many real solutions a quadratic has. The sign of $b^2-4ac$ tells whether there are two, one, or no real solutions. For $x^2 + 8x + 13 = 0$, the discriminant is $\Delta = 64 - 52 = 12$:
$$r, s = \frac{-8 \pm \sqrt{12}}{2} = \frac{-8 \pm 2\sqrt{3}}{2} = -4 \pm \sqrt{3}$$
For $x^2 + 10x + 13 = 0$, the discriminant is $\Delta = 100 - 52 = 48$:
$$t, u = \frac{-10 \pm \sqrt{48}}{2} = \frac{-10 \pm 4\sqrt{3}}{2} = -5 \pm 2\sqrt{3}$$

steps.title: Form the new roots and verify their sum
steps.content:
With $r = -4 - \sqrt{3}$, $s = -4 + \sqrt{3}$, $t = -5 - 2\sqrt{3}$, $u = -5 + 2\sqrt{3}$:
$$r + t = -9 - 3\sqrt{3}, \quad s + u = -9 + 3\sqrt{3}$$
Their sum is $(r+t) + (s+u) = -18$, confirming the $x$ coefficient in $x^2 + 18x + c = 0$.

steps.title: Calculate c as the product of the new roots
steps.content:
$$(r+t)(s+u) = (-9 - 3\sqrt{3})(-9 + 3\sqrt{3})$$
This is a difference of squares $(a-b)(a+b) = a^2 - b^2$ with $a = -9$ and $b = 3\sqrt{3}$:
$$= 81 - 9 \cdot 3 = 81 - 27 = 54$$
Therefore, $c = 54$.
